hypothesis: Incorporating explicit many-body dispersion interactions (beyond pairwise DFT-D3 corrections) into uMLIP training datasets for MOFs will disproportionately improve prediction accuracy for flexible and large-pore MOFs—where guest-free long-range van der Waals interactions dominate structural stability—compared to rigid or small-pore frameworks, such that models trained on many-body dispersion-corrected reference data will reduce bulk modulus mean absolute error by at least 30% specifically for frameworks with largest pore diameters exceeding 15 Å while showing negligible improvement for frameworks below 8 Å pore diameter.
